compiled_state.title: Add ticket delete subcommand with Jira propagation and dependency cleanup
compiled_state.author: Test
compiled_state.description:
## Context
Agents and developers currently have no way to permanently remove tickets that are duplicates, mistakes, or superseded. The only option is `closed`, which keeps tickets visible in Jira and in dependency graphs. A dedicated deletion command is needed that removes tickets from all active views, propagates removal to Jira, and atomically cleans up any blocker dependencies tied to the deleted ticket. Deletion is irreversible — no restore path exists.

## Success Criteria
- `ticket delete <id>` without `--user-approved` exits non-zero with an instructional error message directing the agent to confirm with the user and re-run with `--user-approved`; the `--user-approved` flag is absent from help text
- `ticket delete <id> --user-approved` exits non-zero with a clear error listing any child tickets not in `deleted` status
- `ticket delete <id> --user-approved` on a valid ticket atomically writes a `STATUS(deleted)` event and an `ARCHIVED` event in a single git commit
- After deletion, `ticket list` (default output) excludes the deleted ticket; `ticket show <id>` still works and shows `status: deleted` and `archived: true`
- `ticket transition <id> <current> deleted` exits non-zero with an error directing the user to `ticket delete`
- On the next outbound bridge run, the bridge calls the Jira DELETE API for the ticket's Jira key; all subsequent sync operations for that ticket are skipped (idempotent); if Jira returns 403 (permission denied) the bridge writes a BRIDGE_ALERT and does not retry
- All `depends_on` links where the deleted ticket is the blocking target (`target_id`) are removed via UNLINK events written atomically in the same git commit as the delete events
- `ticket transition <parent-id> <current> closed` does not fail due to child tickets in `deleted` status (`deleted` is added to `CLOSED_STATUSES` in the open-children guard)
- An acceptance test confirms the end-to-end deletion workflow: `ticket delete --user-approved` on a test ticket followed by `ticket list` (excludes it), `ticket graph` (no edges to the deleted ID), and the outbound bridge mock/stub confirms the Jira DELETE API is called with the correct issue key

## Scenario Analysis
- **Concurrent delete and transition race on same ticket** (`high`, `runtime`): Two agents simultaneously attempt to delete and transition the same ticket. The delete script reads the current status, passes the non-deleted-children guard, then the transition script writes a STATUS event. The delete script then writes STATUS(deleted) + ARCHIVED events on top of the transition event.
- **Child ticket deleted between guard check and commit write** (`high`, `runtime`): The non-deleted-children guard passes because all children are in terminal states. Between the guard check and the atomic commit write, a concurrent agent transitions a child ticket back to in_progress. The parent deletion proceeds but the child is now active with a deleted parent.
- **UNLINK events written for stale dependency snapshot** (`high`, `runtime`): The delete script reads depends_on links at guard-check time. A concurrent agent adds a new blocker dependency between read and commit, leaving an active depends_on link pointing to a deleted ticket.
- **Jira DELETE not idempotent on retry after network failure** (`medium`, `runtime`): Bridge calls Jira DELETE; API succeeds but response is lost. On next bridge run, if Jira returns 404 for already-deleted issue and bridge does not treat 404 as success, it marks ticket as needing further sync.
- **ticket list exclusion depends on event ordering not materialized state** (`medium`, `runtime`): If ticket list reads events in git log order rather than materializing final state, a delete event written out-of-order may cause ticket to appear as non-deleted.
- **CLOSED_STATUSES update not backward compatible with older worktrees** (`high`, `deployment`): Worktrees running the old ticket-transition.sh do not include `deleted` in CLOSED_STATUSES, causing parent-close to fail when children are in deleted status.
- **acli-integration.py delete_issue method absent in pinned CI environment** (`high`, `deployment`): CI pipelines pinning acli-integration.py to a specific commit may not have delete_issue, raising AttributeError and halting all Jira sync.
- **Ticket with no Jira key causes bridge to call DELETE on null endpoint** (`medium`, `configuration`): A locally-created ticket never synced to Jira has no jira_key; bridge constructs malformed DELETE URL.
- **Missing JIRA_API_TOKEN at deletion sync time** (`high`, `configuration`): Token absent or expired causes DELETE to fail; if bridge does not flag for retry, ticket remains alive in Jira indefinitely.
- **Jira project configured with restricted delete permissions** (`high`, `configuration`): 403 from Jira on DELETE creates persistent local/Jira divergence without alerting if not handled as BRIDGE_ALERT.

## Dependencies
- Jira service-account permission: The bridge account configured via JIRA_API_TOKEN must hold the "Delete Issues" project permission in Jira for Jira deletion to function. The bridge degrades gracefully to BRIDGE_ALERT on 403. This is an operational prerequisite, not an upstream epic.
- No other epics are prerequisites.

## Approach
New `ticket-delete.sh` script enforces the `--user-approved` gate, non-deleted-children guard, and atomically writes STATUS(deleted) + ARCHIVED + UNLINK events in a single git commit. `ticket-transition.sh` blocks `deleted` as a target status and adds `deleted` to CLOSED_STATUSES. `acli-integration.py` gains a `delete_issue` method using `acli jira workitem delete --key`. The outbound bridge adds a `deleted` status case that calls Jira DELETE and skips all further sync for that ticket.

### Planning Intelligence Log
- **Web research (Step 2.6)**: triggered
  - Bright-line conditions that fired: External integration (Jira DELETE API — acli jira workitem delete syntax, REST API permissions, 403 handling)
- **Scenario analysis (Step 2.75)**: triggered — 10 surviving scenarios (3 filtered by blue team)
  - Scenarios surviving blue team filter: 10
- **Practitioner-requested additional cycles**: none
- **Follow-on scrutiny (Step 0)**: not triggered
- **Feasibility resolution (Step 2.5)**: not triggered
- **LLM-instruction signal (Step 5)**: not triggered

comments.body:
PREPLANNING_CONTEXT: Preplanning complete for epic 6f7a-4ba6 (ticket delete command).

Story map:
- S1 (38fa-6072, P1): ticket-delete.sh -- core delete command, --user-approved gate, STATUS+ARCHIVED+UNLINK events, open-children guard, ticket-graph.py terminal status fix
- S2 (547e-c999, P2): Transition safeguards -- blocked transition target, CLOSED_STATUSES in 3 locations (ticket-transition.sh, ticket-unblock.py, sprint-next-batch.sh)
- S3 (bbe3-bd36, P2): Jira bridge -- delete_issue in acli-integration.py, bridge-outbound intercept at top of STATUS handler, inbound resurrection guard
- S4 (2501-5877, P3): End-to-end acceptance test -- depends on S1+S2+S3
- S5 (2f7e-f4e9, P3): Documentation -- ticket-cli-reference.md + CLAUDE.md updates, depends on S1+S2+S3

Dependencies: S3→S1, S4→(S1,S2,S3), S5→(S1,S2,S3). S1 and S2 are independent and can parallelize.

Escalation policy: Autonomous -- agents proceed with best judgment, document assumptions.

Adversarial review: 2 red team findings accepted by blue team, applied as considerations to S3 (SYNC tombstone concern) and done-def update to S2 (error message consistency).
comments.author: Test

comments.body:
SCOPE AMENDMENT (brainstorm 2026-04-20)

This epic expands beyond the original ticket-delete scope to include two related surface-area changes:

1. NEW VERB SUGAR PATTERN
   - Establish the thin-shim verb pattern: new subcommands implemented as thin bash shims over a shared Python library (the sourceable ticket-lib API from Epic 78fc-3858). The delete verb being added here is the reference implementation of this pattern.
   - Verbs landing in OTHER epics (ticket ready in 2c28-e5b9; ticket claim and ticket messages in 0777-0f3d) will follow the pattern established here.
   - This epic does NOT implement ready/claim/messages — only establishes the shared-library-plus-thin-shim pattern via the delete verb.

2. NEW LINK RELATIONS: duplicates and supersedes
   - Add duplicates and supersedes as net-new link relation values (currently only blocks, depends_on, relates_to exist).
   - duplicates: directional link (source duplicates target); indicates source is a duplicate of target. Closing with resolution=Duplicate points at the target ticket.
   - supersedes: directional link (source supersedes target); indicates target is obsoleted by source. Used when scope expands, plans change, or a redesign replaces prior work.
   - Jira sync for both is lossy: both map to Jira Relates link type (Jira has no native duplicates/supersedes). Comments on the link or ticket explain the nature of the relationship.
   - Local-richer-wins precedence rule on INBOUND: if Jira has a Relates link and local has a supersedes link matching the same pair, the local supersedes value is preserved. Inbound Relates links without local match are imported as relates_to.
   - The actual Jira bridge sync work (Relates bidirectional with precedence rule) is implemented by Epic 83e2-04ae (bridge sync remediation) — this epic only adds the relation values to the local CLI and reducer.

Dependencies:
- Add depends_on on 78fc-3858 (sourceable ticket-lib API is the shared library this epic builds on).

RERUN REQUIRED: preplanning must be rerun for this epic to generate stories covering the new verbs and link relations. Complexity: COMPLEX (new relation values, new CLI surface, reducer schema change).
comments.author: Claude
